title: Cat Pressing Head Firmly Against a Wall: An Urgent Guide
meta_desc: If your cat is pressing its head against a wall, this is a veterinary emergency. Learn about head pressing and why you must seek immediate care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Head pressing is a serious neurological emergency requiring immediate veterinary care to diagnose life-threatening underlying causes like brain injury, toxicity, or organ failure.

What does it mean when my cat is head pressing?

Head pressing is a clinical sign where a cat compulsively leans the top of its head against a wall, corner, or other firm object for an extended period. This is not the same as 'bunting,' where a cat affectionately rubs its face on you. In head pressing, the cat appears stuck, disoriented, or unable to move away from the surface.

Is head pressing a medical emergency for my cat?

Yes. The urgency level for head pressing is High, as this behavior indicates neurological distress or damage to the forebrain and thalamus. Potential life-threatening causes include:

  • Lead poisoning
  • Brain tumors or stroke
  • Metabolic disorders like liver failure
  • Severe head trauma

If you observe this behavior, you should seek emergency veterinary care immediately.

How does a photo or video help with my cat's triage?

Capturing a quick photo or video of your cat's behavior is vital for your veterinarian for several reasons:

  • Cats often experience a surge of adrenaline at the clinic, which may cause them to temporarily stop the behavior.
  • A visual record allows the medical team to see exactly what the cat was doing in its home environment.
  • Visual evidence helps the veterinarian reach a faster and more accurate neurological diagnosis.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Medical problems must be excluded when evaluating abnormal feline behaviors, as they can manifest similarly to compulsive disorders. Conditions such as neuropathic pain, dermatologic conditions, myopathies, and focal seizures can present with signs resembling compulsive behaviors. Additionally, painful conditions and any disease process affecting the neurologic system should be ruled out. A therapeutic response trial for neuropathic pain, seizures, pruritus, or compulsive disorders might be considered as part of the diagnostic process.

Chapter: Behavioral Medicine, Dermatology, Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1574)

faqs.question: How can I tell the difference between head pressing and normal rubbing?
faqs.answer: Normal rubbing is brief, social, and usually involves the cat's cheeks or chin. Head pressing involves the cat firmly pushing the top or front of its head against a hard surface for a long time, often while appearing lethargic or confused.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms associated with head pressing?
faqs.answer: You may also notice your cat pacing in circles, staring blankly into space, changes in pupil size, or a sudden loss of vision.

faqs.question: Can head pressing wait until a scheduled vet appointment?
faqs.answer: No. Because head pressing indicates a serious issue with the brain or nervous system, it requires immediate evaluation at an emergency veterinary hospital.
